subject: general
title: A previously good student has become withdrawn and disengaged
query_text: A previously good student has become withdrawn and disengaged

immediate_steps.title: Immediate Action
steps: Find a quiet, private moment — before class or during break — to gently check in: 'I've noticed you seem a little quiet lately. Everything okay?' Keep it casual, not formal.
Assign this student a small, meaningful classroom role (distributing books, leading a group) — responsibility often rebuilds connection and confidence.
Quietly inform the school counsellor or head teacher if withdrawal continues beyond 1–2 weeks, especially if you notice changes in attendance or hygiene.

classroom_activity.title: Classroom Activity — 'My Voice Matters' Journal
classroom_activity.activity: Give every student a half-page of paper. Ask them to write OR draw: 'One thing that made me happy this week' and 'One thing that felt hard.' Collect anonymously. This normalises sharing feelings and helps you identify struggling students without singling anyone out.
classroom_activity.materials: Half-sheets of paper, pencil — nothing else needed

inclusion_strategy.title: Inclusion Strategy
inclusion_strategy.strategy: Pair this student with a trusted, kind peer for group tasks — not the highest achiever, but someone warm. Peer connection is often the bridge back to engagement. Avoid cold-calling this student publicly until they show signs of re-engagement.

reflection_prompt.title: Reflection
reflection_prompt.question: After your quiet check-in with this student, ask yourself: Did I listen more than I spoke? Sometimes a child just needs to feel seen — not fixed.

source_citation.title: Source
source_citation.reference: NEP 2020, Section 4.6 — Holistic Development and Student Well-being; NISHTHA Module on Social-Emotional Learning in Indian Classrooms
